people.name: Basil I
people.role: Byzantine Emperor, founder of the Macedonian dynasty
people.summary: An Armenian peasant of extraordinary physical strength and ruthless ambition, Basil rose from groom to co-emperor by ingratiating himself with Michael III and then murdering his rivals Bardas and Michael. As sole emperor he proved a transformative ruler, reconquering the Dalmatian coast and Italian cities, rebuilding the navy, reforming the law code, and adorning Constantinople with monumental buildings — leaving the empire, in Brownworth's telling, the strongest power in Europe and founding a stable dynasty that would preside over Byzantium's Golden Age.

people.name: Michael III
people.summary: Brownworth pushes back against Michael's reputation as a buffoon, noting that "there was greatness in his time," but concedes he was a weak ruler who lavished attention on horses and drinking and handed government to favorites. His capriciousness and willingness to let Basil murder his uncle Bardas ultimately sealed his own fate, as Basil turned on him next.

people.name: Bardas
people.role: Caesar, uncle of Michael III, de facto ruler of the Byzantine Empire
people.summary: The real power behind Michael III's throne, Bardas was a man of both charisma and vision who led expeditions into Asia Minor and Armenia, defeated and killed Caliph Omar, and championed Photius as patriarch. His fatal mistake was underestimating Basil, whom he dismissed as a mere drinking companion, and he was cut down by Basil's sword during a campaign in Asia Minor.

people.name: Photius
people.summary: Described by Brownworth as one of the most famous scholars of the Middle Ages, Photius rose rapidly through court ranks — from captain of the guard to chief imperial secretary — before Bardas elevated him to patriarch within a week of joining the priesthood. He championed Byzantine ecclesiastical independence against Pope Nicholas I, commissioned Cyril and Methodius to evangelize the Slavs, and served as the key spiritual force behind Basil's cultural renaissance, though his political machinations around the succession of Leo nearly proved his undoing.

people.name: Empress Theodora
people.role: Byzantine Empress, regent for Michael III
people.summary: Theodora ended the iconoclastic controversy by restoring the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts. She ruled as regent for her son Michael III until he, furious at being excluded from public life and forbidden from seeing his mistress Eudosia, overthrew her and forced her into a monastery.

people.name: Empress Irene
people.summary: Brownworth references Irene as the subject of the previous episode, characterizing her reign as disastrous — marked by the virtual collapse of Byzantine prestige in the West, the creation of a rival empire, and the coronation of Charlemagne as Emperor of the Romans, a direct insult to the Eastern Empire.

people.name: Charlemagne
people.role: Frankish King, first Holy Roman Emperor
people.summary: Mentioned as the rival emperor crowned during Irene's reign, Charlemagne was given the title Emperor of the Romans as a direct insult to the Eastern Empire, creating a rival western empire and contributing to the collapse of Byzantine prestige in the West.

people.name: Ostrogorski
people.summary: Cited by Brownworth to characterize Michael III's reign: "He was not great, but there was greatness in his time." Ostrogorski is invoked to push back against the caricature of Michael as a mere buffoon.

people.name: Caliph Omar
people.summary: The Saracen leader defeated and killed by Bardas during his expeditions into Asia Minor and Armenia, marking the first blow struck against Islam's advance in almost a hundred years.

people.name: Boris I of Bulgaria
people.summary: Boris I sent ambassadors to Constantinople asking for Christian teachers, an opportunity that Photius exploited by dispatching Cyril and Methodius. He later played Rome against Constantinople over Bulgaria's ecclesiastical allegiance, but Basil outmaneuvered him by referring the question to the three eastern bishoprics, who predictably assigned Bulgaria to Constantinople.

people.name: Constantine (Cyril)
people.role: Byzantine monk, missionary, creator of the Cyrillic alphabet
people.summary: Commissioned by Photius along with his brother Methodius to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus, Constantine took the Slavic name Cyril and, recognizing that the Slavs lacked a written language, devised the Cyrillic alphabet based on Greek characters. Though the mission to the Moravians failed, his alphabetic creation became the basis of writing systems for most of Eastern Europe.

people.name: Methodius
people.summary: Brother of Constantine (Cyril), commissioned by Photius alongside him to evangelize the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, he and his brother did their work well overall, pulling Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere.

people.name: Pope Nicholas I
people.summary: The pope whose claim to universal spiritual authority Photius was determined to challenge. Nicholas protested when Michael III deposed the previous patriarch and installed Photius without papal consultation; Photius responded by telling him to mind his own business. Later, when Basil deposed Photius to gain papal support, the pope had already been succeeded by a pontiff furious over Bulgaria.

people.name: Eudosia
people.role: Mistress of Michael III, later wife of Basil I and Empress
people.summary: Originally the mistress of Michael III, who had been forced to give her up in his youth. Michael later forced Basil to divorce his first wife and marry Eudosia so he could keep her close to court, with it being common rumor that her first child with Basil, Leo, was actually Michael's son. After Michael's murder, Basil crowned her empress.

people.name: Leo VI
people.role: Byzantine Emperor, son of Basil I
people.summary: Widely rumored to be the biological son of Michael III rather than Basil I, Leo was despised by Basil, who imprisoned and threatened to blind him after he refused to give up his mistress Zoe. Photius feared Leo's succession because it would cost him power. Brownworth strongly implies Leo was complicit in Basil's mysterious hunting death, since the rescue party was led by the father of Leo's own mistress.

people.name: Constantine
people.role: Co-emperor, eldest son of Basil I
people.summary: The only son of Basil's first wife and the one person Basil ever truly loved. His unexpected early death threw Basil into a lasting depression that bordered on insanity, and Photius exploited the emperor's grief — holding a séance and even canonizing Constantine — to block the succession of the hated Leo.

people.name: Zoe
people.summary: Leo VI's mistress whom he refused to give up, causing Basil to beat him severely. Her father led the hunting rescue party, which Brownworth suggests implicates Leo in the suspicious circumstances of Basil's death.

people.name: Nikephoros Phokas the Elder
people.summary: Basil's brilliant general who, using Otranto and Bari as a bridgehead, would go on to conquer virtually all of southern Italy before the end of the century, cementing Byzantine dominance in the Italian south.

people.name: Basil II
people.summary: Mentioned at the episode's close as Basil I's great-great-grandson who, a century later, would bring the empire to its height of prestige and glory — the subject of the next episode.

people.name: Justinian I
people.summary: Invoked repeatedly as the benchmark against whom Basil I measured himself. Basil saw himself as a new Justinian, determined to reconquer western territories, revise the law code, and undertake massive building projects — including repairing Justinian's great church the Hagia Sophia and rebuilding the Church of the Holy Apostles that Justinian had restored.

people.name: Constantine the Great
people.summary: Referenced as the founder of the Church of the Holy Apostles, the traditional burial place of the emperors, which Justinian later rebuilt and Basil I restored again.

places.name: Constantinople
places.summary: The capital of the Byzantine Empire and the central stage for this episode's court intrigues, murders, and building programs. Basil had ambitious plans to turn it into a vast treasure house through massive construction, including repairs to the Hagia Sophia, a rebuilt Church of the Holy Apostles, new public baths, a treasury, and his crowning achievement the Nea Ekklesia.

places.name: Hagia Sophia
places.summary: Justinian's great church, where Basil swore falsely to Bardas that he bore him no ill will, signing in blood from a reliquary. Later Basil repaired the damaged western arch — which had been struck by an earthquake and was threatening to collapse — and added new mosaics.

places.name: Church of the Holy Apostles
places.summary: The traditional burial place of the Byzantine emperors, founded by Constantine and then rebuilt by Justinian. Basil I completely rebuilt it again, covering it in mosaic, marble, and gold.

places.name: Nea Ekklesia
places.summary: Basil's supreme architectural triumph and his answer to Justinian's Hagia Sophia. Built within the palace grounds and dedicated to the Virgin Mary, Saint Nicholas, Saint Michael, and Elijah, it featured cascading gold-covered domes visible far out to sea and an interior encrusted with gold, silver, and precious stones. No trace of it remains today.

places.name: Golden Horn
places.summary: The waterway Basil crossed immediately after murdering Michael III to reach the great palace and secure a smooth transfer of power.

places.name: Asia Minor
places.summary: The theatre of multiple military campaigns in this episode: Bardas led two expeditions here against the Saracens, Basil and the army were crossing through Asia Minor when Bardas was murdered, and Basil later swept through it personally to defeat the Paulicians and capture Tefrike.

places.name: Armenia
places.summary: The homeland from which Basil's family had been relocated to the Byzantine province of Macedonia, and one of the territories where Bardas led campaigns against the Saracens.

places.name: Macedonia
places.summary: The Byzantine province to which Basil's family had been relocated, probably from Armenia. This is the reason his successors were called the Macedonian dynasty, even though Basil himself was of Armenian origin.

places.name: Bulgaria
places.summary: Basil spent part of his childhood as a Bulgarian prisoner of war before escaping in his mid-twenties. Later, as emperor, he outmaneuvered both Boris I and the pope over Bulgaria's ecclesiastical allegiance, ensuring it fell into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere — where it remains to this day.

places.name: Crete
places.summary: Crete had been taken over by the Saracens and become a foremost nest of piracy. Bardas was planning an expedition against it when he was murdered by Basil, and the expedition subsequently came to nothing even after Basil became sole emperor.

places.name: Sicily
places.summary: One of the western territories Bardas failed to hold against Saracen invasion. Basil also failed to defend it, and its last imperial stronghold of Syracuse fell in 878.

places.name: Syracuse
places.summary: The last imperial stronghold in Sicily, it fell to the Saracens in 878, marking the end of Byzantine rule on the island.

places.name: Dalmatia
places.summary: Basil met spectacular success by invading the Dalmatian coast, expelling the Saracens and returning it to imperial control.

places.name: Otranto
places.summary: An Italian coastal city reconquered by Basil I, which along with Bari provided an excellent bridgehead from which Nikephoros Phokas would later conquer virtually all of southern Italy.

places.name: Bari
places.summary: An Italian coastal city reconquered by Basil I alongside Otranto, providing the bridgehead for the subsequent Byzantine reconquest of virtually all of southern Italy.

places.name: Tefrike
places.summary: The main stronghold of the Paulician heretics, captured by Basil during his personal campaign through Asia Minor. With its fall, the Paulician threat to the empire was effectively neutralized.

places.name: Samosata
places.summary: An important fortress captured by Basil's brother during the decade of slow but steady advances against the Saracens.

places.name: Zepetra
places.summary: An important fortress captured alongside Samosata by Basil's brother during the sustained campaign against the Saracens.

places.name: Baghdad
places.summary: Destination of an embassy in which Photius participated on his way up through the court ranks, before Bardas elevated him to patriarch.

places.name: Rome
places.summary: The seat of the papacy and the western rival to Constantinople's ecclesiastical authority. Photius challenged Rome's claim to universal spiritual authority, and Basil later played the pope against himself over Bulgaria before recalling Photius.

places.name: Alexandria
places.summary: One of the three supposedly neutral eastern bishoprics Basil referred the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to; as an eastern see, it predictably ruled in favor of Constantinople.

places.name: Antioch
places.summary: One of the three supposedly neutral eastern bishoprics Basil referred the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to; as an eastern see, it predictably ruled in favor of Constantinople.

places.name: Jerusalem
places.summary: One of the three supposedly neutral eastern bishoprics Basil referred the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to; as an eastern see, it predictably ruled in favor of Constantinople.

events.name: Coronation of Charlemagne as Emperor of the Romans
events.summary: Charlemagne was crowned Emperor of the Romans during Irene's reign, intended as a direct insult to the Eastern Empire and marking a major blow to Byzantine prestige in the West by creating a rival empire.

events.name: End of Iconoclasm
events.summary: The iconoclastic controversy, which had flared up again after Irene's fall, was finally ended by the Empress Theodora, who restored the veneration of icons and was sainted for her efforts.

events.name: Rus Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: A group of Scandinavian and Slavic raiders who called themselves the Rus besieged Constantinople — not exactly threatening to the city, but giving everyone a good scare. Photius saw in this event the opportunity to send missionaries to the Rus.

events.name: Mission of Cyril and Methodius to the Slavs
events.summary: Photius commissioned two monks, Constantine (Cyril) and Methodius, to bring Christianity to the Bulgarians, Moravians, and Rus. Though unsuccessful with the Moravians, they brought Bulgaria firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere, and Constantine's creation of the Cyrillic alphabet became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

events.name: Creation of the Cyrillic Alphabet
events.summary: Realizing the Slavic peoples lacked a written alphabet, Constantine (Cyril) devised one based on Greek characters, which was named Cyrillic after him. It became the basis for writing systems across most of Eastern Europe.

events.name: Conversion of Bulgaria to Orthodox Christianity
events.summary: Bulgaria was pulled firmly into the Byzantine Orthodox sphere of influence through the mission of Cyril and Methodius and later confirmed by Basil's diplomatic maneuver routing the Bulgarian ecclesiastical question to friendly eastern bishoprics. Brownworth notes this allegiance endures to this day.

events.name: Photian Schism
events.summary: Photius engineered a schism between the Eastern and Western churches by being installed as patriarch without papal consultation, then telling Pope Nicholas I to mind his own business and converting the Slavs to the Eastern rite, further challenging papal authority. Basil subsequently resolved it by deposing Photius and recognizing papal supremacy — though he ensured the submission was largely nominal.

events.name: Murder of Bardas
events.summary: During a military campaign crossing Asia Minor, Basil drew his sword and killed Bardas with one blow as Bardas was turning to address the emperor. Michael III, who likely knew of the plot, made no protest and immediately wrote to Photius declaring Bardas guilty of treason.

events.name: Basil Crowned Co-Emperor
events.summary: Following the murder of Bardas, Michael returned to Constantinople with the army and crowned Basil co-emperor — a remarkable elevation for the Armenian peasant-turned-groom.

events.name: Murder of Michael III
events.summary: Fearing for his safety when Michael began favoring a new courtier, Basil slipped out of a banquet, bent back the bolts on the imperial bedroom door so it could not be locked, then entered with followers after Michael slept, hacked off the emperor's hands, and had him finished off. He then crossed the Golden Horn to secure the palace, and the murder was greeted with remarkably little public outrage.

events.name: First Deposition of Photius
events.summary: Basil's first act as sole ruler was to depose Photius, sacrificing his patriarch to gain papal support for his western ambitions. He ensured that he himself, not papal delegates, ran the council that passed judgment, keeping the submission largely nominal.

events.name: Bulgarian Ecclesiastical Question
events.summary: Boris I's ambassadors in Constantinople posed a simple question: now that Bulgaria had converted, did it belong ecclesiastically to Rome or Constantinople? Basil deflected by referring the question to the supposedly neutral but actually eastern bishoprics of Alexandria, Antioch, and Jerusalem, who predictably assigned Bulgaria to Constantinople.

events.name: Basil's Campaign Against the Paulicians
events.summary: Personally leading the bulk of his army through Asia Minor, Basil captured the city of Tefrike, the main Paulician stronghold, and killed their leader, effectively neutralizing this heretical sect that had been harassing his eastern border.

events.name: Saracen Campaigns of Basil I
events.summary: The next ten years after the Paulician campaign saw a continual if slow advance against the Saracens, with Basil's brother capturing the important fortresses of Samosata and Zepetra.

events.name: Expedition Against Saracen Crete
events.summary: Bardas was planning this expedition to retake Crete from the Saracens when he was murdered by Basil. The expedition was called off, and Basil's later attempt also came to nothing.

events.name: Fall of Syracuse
events.summary: The last imperial stronghold in Sicily, Syracuse fell to the Saracens in 878, completing the loss of the island from Byzantine control despite Basil's efforts to defend it.

events.name: Byzantine Reconquest of Dalmatia
events.summary: Basil achieved spectacular success by invading the Dalmatian coast, expelling the Saracens and returning it to imperial control.

events.name: Reconquest of Otranto and Bari
events.summary: Basil reconquered the Italian coastal cities of Otranto and Bari, giving his brilliant general Nikephoros Phokas an excellent bridgehead from which he would go on to conquer virtually all of southern Italy before the end of the century.

events.name: Restoration of Photius as Patriarch
events.summary: Realizing that Photius had been sacrificed in vain — the pope furious over Bulgaria negating any expected benefit — Basil recalled him to the capital, entrusted the education of his children to him, and when the patriarchal office became vacant, had him elected again. Photius's influence was immediately felt in increased missionary ties to the Balkan Slavic tribes.

events.name: Compilation of the Prochiron
events.summary: Imitating Justinian the lawgiver, Basil announced he would revise Justinian's law code. Though the project was never finished in his lifetime, he produced the Prochiron — a handbook collecting the most important and applicable legislation, plus a summary of imperial and patriarchal rights. It gave judges a vastly simplified alternative to wading through Justinian's complex Digest.

events.name: Death of Constantine, Son of Basil I
events.summary: The unexpected death of Basil's beloved eldest son Constantine — the only person Basil ever truly loved — threw the emperor into a lasting depression that eventually verged on insanity, and opened the succession question that Photius would exploit to try to block Leo.

events.name: Imprisonment of Leo VI by Basil I
events.summary: Urged on by Photius, who feared Leo's succession would cost him power, and in a state of near-insanity following Constantine's death, Basil imprisoned Leo and threatened to put out his eyes. Public pressure soon forced his release, but the episode cannot have improved relations between father and son.

events.name: Death of Basil I
events.summary: Basil died in 886 in mysterious circumstances. The official story was that a white deer caught its antlers in his belt and dragged him sixteen miles during a hunt, rupturing his stomach; he died nine days later. Brownworth finds this implausible given Basil's legendary strength and the advanced age at which he was supposedly hunting alone, suggesting Leo was likely involved and the hunt was a cover story.
